% === CP437 (IBM/DOS) glyph chart (auto-generated) ===
\chapter{Font Glyphs: CP437 (IBM/DOS)}
\label{sec:font-cp437}

The default font (slot~0).  This is the standard IBM Code Page~437 character set used by the original IBM~PC and compatibles.  Characters \$00--\$1F contain symbols (smileys, card suits, arrows, musical notes) rather than control codes.  Characters \$80--\$FF include accented letters, line-drawing box characters, Greek letters, and mathematical symbols.

Each cell shows the glyph for one character code.  Rows are the high
nibble; columns are the low nibble.
For example, character \$41 is in row~4, column~1.
Select this font with \texttt{FONT 0} or
\texttt{POKE \$A007, 0}.

\subsection*{Characters \$00--\$7F}

% === PETSCII Uppercase glyph chart (auto-generated) ===
\chapter{Font Glyphs: PETSCII Uppercase}
\label{sec:font-petscii-upper}

The Commodore~64 character set~1 (slot~1, uppercase mode).  Standard ASCII letters appear in uppercase.  Shift+letter produces graphic symbols --- line segments, blocks, checkerboards, card suits, and geometric shapes --- in the \$80--\$9F and \$A0--\$BF ranges.  These are the iconic PETSCII graphic characters used in countless C64 programs and demos.

Each cell shows the glyph for one character code.  Rows are the high
nibble; columns are the low nibble.
For example, character \$41 is in row~4, column~1.
Select this font with \texttt{FONT 1} or
\texttt{POKE \$A007, 1}.

\subsection*{Characters \$00--\$7F}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[x=1mm, y=-1mm]
  \node[font=\scriptsize\ttfamily\bfseries,retroblue] at (9.40,3.52) {\$0};
  \node[font=\scriptsize\ttfamily\bfseries,retroblue] at (18.20,3.52) {\$1};
  \node[font=\scriptsize\ttfamily\bfseries,retroblue] at (27.00,3.52) {\$2};

% === PETSCII Lowercase glyph chart (auto-generated) ===
\chapter{Font Glyphs: PETSCII Lowercase}
\label{sec:font-petscii-lower}

The Commodore~64 character set~2 (slot~2, lowercase mode).  Unshifted letters produce lowercase; Shift+letter produces uppercase.  The graphic symbols from character set~1 remain accessible at the same code points.  This mode is selected by the C64's Shift+Commodore key combination.

Each cell shows the glyph for one character code.  Rows are the high
nibble; columns are the low nibble.
For example, character \$41 is in row~4, column~1.
Select this font with \texttt{FONT 2} or
\texttt{POKE \$A007, 2}.

\subsection*{Characters \$00--\$7F}
